EMEA Finance Team · Beginner Session · 60–90 min

🧮 Finance × AI:
From “What is AI?” to Copilot, Confidently

An entertaining, no-jargon first session for accountants: what AI actually is, why Microsoft 365 Copilot beats a plain chatbot for finance work, where agentic assistants fit — and how to use all of it without giving your auditor a heart attack.

👥 Audience: Finance / Accounting / Controllership 🎚️ Level: Beginner 🎤 Trainer: Çağlar Şahingöz 🧷 Golden rule: AI drafts, humans decide
Focus first ➜ ✍️ The Prompt Formula 🐣 Chatbot vs Agent 📊 Accountant Use Cases 🛡️ Responsible Use
Agenda AI basics Copilot Chatbot vs agent Basic vs M365 Use cases M365 apps Prompt lab Safe use 🎮 Games Workshop Slides Sources
Section 01

The agenda — a story in seven acts 🎬

  1. Warm-up — “Where do we lose time?” Capture real examples: email chains, reconciliations, variance narratives, policy look-ups, meeting actions. (Spoiler: everyone says emails.)
  2. AI in plain English: models, prompts, context, outputs, limitations — no maths required.
  3. Copilot overview: web chat, M365 Copilot, app integrations and enterprise data context.
  4. Chatbot vs agentic: answer generator → task assistant → governed finance agent.
  5. Accountant demos: Excel, Word, PowerPoint, Outlook and Teams.
  6. Prompt practice: the team rewrites weak prompts into finance-ready prompts.
  7. Responsible use: confidentiality, source checking, human judgement and escalation.

🏁 You leave knowing…

  • ✅ When to use AI — and when absolutely not.
  • ✅ Why M365 Copilot is more useful for finance than a generic chatbot.
  • ✅ The prompt skeleton: role + task + context + format + constraints.
  • ✅ Safe accountant use cases that never bypass review or controls.
Section 02

What is AI, really? 🤖

Artificial intelligence is software designed to perform tasks that normally need human-like reasoning, language understanding, pattern recognition or decision support.

Generative AI is the branch that creates new content: text, tables, summaries, code, slides, analysis drafts. It doesn’t “know” things the way you do — it predicts useful output from patterns plus the context you give it.

🧠 Finance analogy: think of AI as a blisteringly fast graduate trainee on day one — superb at drafting, summarising, comparing and structuring, but you would never let them post a journal unreviewed. Every accounting judgement, number and control conclusion still needs your sign-off.

Five words that explain everything 📖

TermPlain EnglishFinance example
PromptYour instruction to the AI.“Draft a variance commentary for May actuals vs forecast using this table.”
ContextThe information the AI can see.Entity, period, currency, policy extract, data table, audience.
ModelThe engine generating the answer.Sits behind Copilot, reasoning over your request.
Hallucination 👻A confident but wrong answer.Inventing a GL code, policy rule or tax treatment. The scariest ghost in finance.
Grounding ⚓Anchoring answers to real sources.The answer cites the policy, email, workbook or transcript it used.
Section 03

What is Microsoft 365 Copilot? 🚁

Microsoft 365 Copilot brings generative AI directly into Microsoft 365 apps — Outlook, Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Teams and Edge — with enterprise-grade access aligned to Korn Ferry’s standards for data protection and responsible use. Source: M365 Copilot activation email, lines 14–18.

For a finance team, the magic isn’t a new chat window — it’s that Copilot works inside the tools where the work already lives: email threads, meeting recaps, Excel workbooks, Word memos, PowerPoint packs and Teams conversations.

🔑 Key training message: Copilot is not “another chatbot”. With the right licence and permissions, it can use your work context and help inside the Microsoft 365 workflow itself.

🚀 Where to start

  • Sign in with your Korn Ferry account.
  • In web Copilot, look for the “Work” indicator to confirm you’re operating in the Korn Ferry environment. Source: activation email, lines 53–57.
  • Borrow ideas from the prompt library / Prompt Gallery. Source: M365 activation email, lines 32–36; Excel email, lines 81–93.
Section 04

Chatbot → Copilot → Agent: the evolution 🐣➡️🦅

🐣Level 1

Simple chatbot

You ask, it answers. A quick junior drafter with zero access to your files.

🦉Level 2

Copilot with work context

A junior drafter who can also look at your permitted emails, files, meetings and chats.

🦅Level 3

Agentic Copilot

A trained workflow assistant with templates, guardrails, citations and escalation rules.

ModeWhat it feels likeTypical accounting useRisk control
Simple chatbotYou ask; it answers or drafts.Explain a concept, draft an email, summarise a paragraph.Check accuracy; never paste sensitive data into unapproved tools.
Copilot with work contextYou ask; it uses permitted M365 context — emails, files, meetings, chats.Summarise latest emails/files from a person; prep for a meeting; draft from a Word doc.Use only inside the enterprise environment and verify source links.
Agentic CopilotA governed assistant follows a workflow, asks clarifying questions, retrieves sources, uses templates/calculators and escalates when needed.SOX Q&A, accrual support pack, variance commentary, reconciliation checklist, SAP/BOBJ report helper.Clear scope, citations, human-in-the-loop, audit trail — no final judgement without review.
Section 05

Basic Copilot vs M365 Copilot licence ⚖️

AreaBasic / web-styleM365 enterprise-style
ContextMostly what you type or paste.Combines your prompt with permitted work data and M365 app context.
AppsOften a standalone chat experience.Embedded in Outlook, Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Teams and Edge. Source: activation email, lines 14–16.
Security postureDepends on the tool and login context.Enterprise-grade access aligned with Korn Ferry data protection and responsible use. Source: activation email, line 18.
Finance valueGood for generic explanations and first drafts.Better for daily finance work: email summaries, meeting actions, Excel analysis, Word memos, PowerPoint packs.

Why the enterprise licence matters 📝

The productivity case in plain terms: M365 Copilot helps reduce context-switching across document authoring, modelling, research, communication and project coordination — with everyday examples in Word, Outlook, Teams, Excel and PowerPoint. Just as importantly, operating within the Microsoft 365 environment supports confidentiality requirements better than free-tier or third-party tools. Source: internal M365 Copilot licence business-case documentation.

Trainer note: "For accountants, the value is not magic. It is removing friction: summarise faster, draft faster, structure analysis faster — and spend more time reviewing judgement areas."
Section 06

Accountant use cases: safe, practical, real 📊

🧾 Close & reporting

  • Draft monthly close status email.
  • Turn open items into a tracker.
  • Summarise close meeting actions.
  • Create a reviewer checklist for accrual support.

📈 Excel analysis

  • Explain drivers in a variance table.
  • Suggest formula logic.
  • Create conditional formatting and charts.
  • Build an XLOOKUP example and explain it.

🔍 Audit & controls

  • Prepare evidence request summaries.
  • Draft control narratives for review.
  • Flag missing support documents.
  • Generate questions for process owners.

🏛️ Tax / statutory

  • Summarise consultant emails.
  • Convert document lists into status tables.
  • Draft follow-up notes to EY/PwC.
  • Compare requested vs provided support.

🗣️ Stakeholder comms

  • Rewrite technical points for non-finance audiences.
  • Draft concise leadership updates.
  • Prepare meeting agendas.
  • Summarise decisions and next steps.

🎓 Learning & coaching

  • Explain IFRS/US GAAP concepts in plain English.
  • Create mini quizzes.
  • Turn policies into quick reference guides.
  • Create prompt cards for team members.
Section 07

Copilot in Excel — the headline act 🟩

Microsoft’s Excel example says Copilot can go beyond suggestions and summaries when you allow it to edit the file; with your direction, it can make changes using Excel’s built-in tools, and you can review, keep, undo or refine those changes. Source: Excel email, lines 10–16 and 31–39.

🎭 Live demo idea

Use this table. Create a variance bridge by entity and account. Highlight variances greater than 10%. Draft a short commentary for a Regional Financial Controller explaining the top 3 movements and questions to ask the local finance manager.

🏋️ Starter exercise

Use a dummy table with entity, account, actual, forecast, prior month and comments. Ask Copilot to create conditional formatting, a chart, and a commentary draft.

Word, PowerPoint, Outlook & Teams 🎪

📄 Word — the memo machine

Draft accounting memos, policy summaries, audit responses and stakeholder updates. Always ask for headings, assumptions, open questions and reviewer notes.

📽️ PowerPoint — first drafts in minutes

Presentation-ready first drafts: close update, entity risk heatmap, audit status pack or finance transformation update. Ask for slide titles, speaker notes and a clear call to action.

📬 Outlook — thread tamer

Draft emails and summarise long threads. Microsoft’s email suggests prompts such as drafting an email to a person about a topic, and reviewing what someone has sent or shared across emails, chats and files. Source: Explore M365 Copilot email, lines 28–32 and 101–107.

🎥 Teams — never miss a meeting again

Use Recap for missed meetings and ask for detailed summaries, decisions and follow-up actions; Microsoft’s email includes prompts for meeting summaries and decisions/open items. Source: Explore M365 Copilot email, lines 61–67 and 81–87.

Section 08

The Prompt Formula — your new T-account ✍️

Just as every journal entry needs a debit and a credit, every good prompt needs five lines:

Act as [role]. Task: [what you want]. Context: [entity, period, currency, source table/document, audience]. Constraints: [do not invent numbers, cite sources, flag assumptions, ask questions if missing data]. Output format: [email / table / memo / slide outline / checklist].

🃏 Prompt cards to steal

Variance commentary Act as a finance controller. Using the table below, draft a concise month-end variance commentary for regional leadership. Focus on movements over 10% or USD 25k. Do not invent explanations; where the driver is unclear, write a question for the local finance manager. Output as: Executive summary, top drivers, risks, follow-up questions.
Audit support tracker Act as an audit coordinator. Convert the below email/request into a tracker with columns: request, entity, owner, due date, evidence needed, status, risk if delayed. Flag unclear items and draft a polite follow-up email.
Policy Q&A Act as a careful accounting policy assistant. Answer the question using only the policy extract provided. If the extract is insufficient, say what is missing and suggest who should confirm. Include assumptions and a reviewer note.

Weak ➜ Strong: tap to flip 🔄

Click each card to see the upgraded prompt. (This is also your workshop format.)

😴 Weak

“Summarise this.”

Tap to upgrade ➜
💪 Strong

“Summarise this for a finance manager, separating decisions, actions, risks and missing information.”

😴 Weak

“Check my Excel.”

Tap to upgrade ➜
💪 Strong

“Review the logic in this table, identify formula inconsistencies, explain likely causes, and list items requiring human review.”

😴 Weak

“Write an email.”

Tap to upgrade ➜
💪 Strong

“Draft a polite but firm follow-up to the tax adviser requesting missing support by Friday; include a table of outstanding items.”

Human review required
Section 09

Responsible AI rules for the team 🛡️

  • Review outputs: Korn Ferry guidance says AI-generated outputs should be carefully reviewed before use. Source: activation email, lines 42–44.
  • Use business purpose: Korn Ferry systems should be used for legitimate business purposes. Source: IT Security Policy, lines 71–77.
  • Protect confidential data: confidential data includes information whose unauthorised disclosure would likely damage Korn Ferry or affiliates; internal distribution is limited to those with a legitimate business need. Source: IT Security Policy, lines 237–246.
  • Avoid unapproved external tools: external applications or web services must not be used for processing, storage or transmission of Korn Ferry Information Assets unless approved by the Office of the CISO. Source: IT Security Policy, lines 152–154.
  • Meeting AI tools need care: Copilot meeting summaries are based on transcript data, and verbal consent is required before enabling transcription. Source: activation email, lines 45–51.

The finance “never do” list 🚫

  • ❌ Do not ask AI to bypass SOX controls or approval workflows.
  • ❌ Do not let AI make final accounting, tax, legal or audit judgements.
  • ❌ Do not paste client confidential or personal data into unapproved tools.
  • ❌ Do not accept invented GL codes, policy references, tax rates or dates.
  • ❌ Do not use AI output externally without review and source checking.

The internal finance AI guide recommends a RAG-first, cite-first approach, scoped use cases, tools/templates for numbers, human-in-the-loop escalation and permissions by default. Source: Finance AI Copilot guide, lines 153–160.

Bonus · Section 09½

🎮 The Game Arcade — for shy accountants only

Three 60-second games, all played by anonymous voting — nobody speaks, nobody is put on the spot. Run each with the thinking clock below, collect votes via a Teams poll, then hit “Reveal”. Accountants love finding errors; it’s literally the job.

⏱️ The Thinking Clock — featuring “Pencil Down” 🎵

A Countdown-style timer scored with “Pencil Down” — an original composition by your trainer, embedded right in this page (no internet, no files, no copyright worries). Start a round and the music plays automatically; it ends as pencils go down. Prefer silence-with-ticks? Toggle the music off and the synthesised ticking takes over.

60

🔍 Game 1 — Spot the Source: real or fabricated?

Five Copilot-style citations. Vote per item: does this source actually exist? Lesson: AI can cite things that sound real but aren’t — always click through.

“…per the Korn Ferry IT Security Policy.”
Reveal

✅ Real — it’s the same policy cited throughout this training.

“…per IFRS 17.4.2 Addendum on Intercompany Netting.”
Reveal

👻 Fabricated — IFRS 17 is about insurance contracts, and no such addendum exists. It sounds plausible, which is exactly the danger.

“…per IAS 21 — The Effects of Changes in Foreign Exchange Rates.”
Reveal

✅ Real — a genuine IFRS standard, and one this team uses constantly.

“…per US GAAP ASC 606-99, ‘Quick Revenue Shortcut’.”
Reveal

👻 Fabricated — ASC 606 is real (revenue recognition), but there is no subtopic 99 and certainly no “quick shortcut”. If a citation promises a shortcut, be suspicious twice.

“…per the M365 Copilot activation email (Korn Ferry IT rollout comms).”
Reveal

✅ Real — the rollout email every licensed user received. Bonus lesson: a few people will vote “fabricated” on real sources — over-suspicion is also a calibration error.

🎰 Game 2 — The Confidence Meter

For each scenario, vote: 🟢 Trust it · 🟡 Verify first · 🔴 Suspect hallucination. The winner is the best-calibrated voter — not the most cynical one. Lesson: review intensity should match risk, not paranoia.

Copilot states a specific tax rate for a Middle East jurisdiction — from memory, with no source cited.
Reveal

🔴 Suspect / always verify. Unsourced + numeric + jurisdiction-specific is the classic hallucination danger zone — rates change, and a wrong rate in finance is not a typo, it’s a finding. Confirm against current official guidance or your advisers.

Copilot summarises a Teams meeting you attended, citing the transcript, with links to the recap.
Reveal

🟡 Verify-light. It’s grounded in a real transcript, so structure is usually solid — but skim the names, owners and deadlines before forwarding. Grounding reduces risk; it doesn’t remove your review.

Copilot explains the difference between an accrual and a prepayment in plain English, for a new joiner.
Reveal

🟢 Mostly trust. Conceptual, low-stakes, instantly self-verifiable by any accountant in the room. This is exactly the kind of task to delegate freely.

Copilot produces an end-of-service benefit accrual formula for a Gulf entity — no policy or legislation cited.
Reveal

🔴 Suspect. Statutory calculations vary by jurisdiction, contract type and service length — and they change. This must be tied to the actual labour law text or policy before any journal touches it.

🏆 Game 3 — The Worst Prompt Awards

Four anonymised prompts “submitted by colleagues” (replace with real anonymous pre-session submissions via a Forms link for maximum laughs). Vote for the worst — then rewrite the winner together using the Section 08 formula. Lesson: the worst prompt usually reveals the most common workflow.

A) “fix this”
B) “make it nice for the boss”
C) “do the recon”
D) “urgent help asap pls”
Reveal: the group rewrite (example for “do the recon”)
Act as a senior accountant. Task: review this bank reconciliation extract and list unmatched items by age and amount. Context: [entity], [period], [currency]; audience is the reviewing controller. Constraints: do not invent numbers; flag anything you cannot match and ask questions where data is missing. Output: a table of open items plus three reviewer questions.

📡 How to run this over a Teams call

StepWhat you doWhy it works for shy people
1. Pre-build pollsBefore the call, create one Teams Poll per game round (Meeting chat → ➕ Apps → Polls/Forms → multiple choice, e.g. “Real / Fabricated”). Keep “Record names” OFF so results stay anonymous.Anonymous = honest votes, zero exposure.
2. Share with soundScreen-share this page and tick “Include computer sound” in the Teams share tray — the thinking clock’s ticking will play for everyone.The clock creates urgency and fun without anyone having to talk.
3. Run a roundShow the claim → launch the poll → hit Start 60s. When the chime sounds, close the poll, read the split aloud (“60/40 — interesting!”), click the in-page buttons to mirror the tally, then hit Reveal.The reveal does the teaching — the discussion happens about the result, never about a person.
4. No-poll fallbackUse Teams reactions instead: 👍 = Real/Trust, ❤️ = Verify, 😂 = Fabricated/Suspect. Count them live.One click, no typing, still nobody speaks.
5. PrizesBest-calibrated voter (Game 2) and best rewrite (Game 3) get bragging rights — and their prompt enters the team prompt library.Recognition without forced presentation.

⚠️ Assumption flagged: Teams Polls/Forms availability depends on your tenant settings — test one poll in a dry-run meeting before the session. The in-page vote buttons are a presenter tally tool only; they do not collect attendee votes.

Section 10

Mini workshop: the 20-minute Prompt Olympics 🏅

  1. Pick a real workflow: month-end email, tax evidence request, variance pack, accrual support, meeting recap.
  2. Write a weak prompt: what someone would naturally type first. (No judgement — we’ve all typed “fix this”.)
  3. Improve it: add role, context, constraints and output format.
  4. Run it in Copilot: use dummy or sanitised data when practising.
  5. Review as an accountant: what is accurate, unsupported, risky or missing?
  6. Share one prompt: build the team prompt library — your collective gold reserve. 🪙

Section 12

Source notes & flagged assumptions 📚

  • M365 Copilot activation/welcome email (standard Korn Ferry IT rollout comms sent to licensed users): app availability, enterprise-grade access, responsible use, Teams transcript consent, “Work” indicator, learning path and support contacts.
  • Microsoft 365 Copilot update emails (Microsoft product comms): work-data context, meeting recap prompts, email draft prompt, Excel editing example and Prompt Gallery references.
  • Korn Ferry IT Security Policy: legitimate business use, confidential/protected data, approved systems, external applications, secure storage/transmission principles.
  • Internal finance AI Copilot guide: multi-agent finance design, RAG-first/cite-first approach, human-in-the-loop, permissions by default, phased rollout and finance use cases.
⚠️ Assumptions flagged by the trainer (i.e. me):
  • All line-number citations are carried over unchanged from the original training draft’s source documents — they have not been independently re-verified in this redesign. Personal/request-specific email references have been generalised to internal documentation for the wider EMEA audience.
  • No statistics, percentages or productivity figures have been invented or added; the only numeric thresholds shown (e.g. “>10% or USD 25k”) come from the original prompt cards.
  • Copilot feature behaviour can change with Microsoft updates — confirm against current Korn Ferry guidance before each session.
  • Emoji and humour are training flavour only and carry no policy weight. 😄

This is a training aid, not a policy document. For compliance, legal, security or accounting judgement questions, use official Korn Ferry policies and designated subject matter experts.

Built for the EMEA Finance team · Drafts by AI, decisions by humans · v2 redesign
